reasoning: The most recent value of the national average savings account interest rate (SNDR) was 0.400. In late October 2025, the Federal Reserve cut the federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points (its second consecutive 25 bps cut). Savings deposit rates, particularly high-yield savings accounts which heavily influence the weighted average, tend to decrease in response to Fed rate cuts. Because of this strong downward pressure, the probability that the national average savings rate will strictly *increase* from its current 0.40% by December or February is very low. The rate may remain flat (as it did between September and November) or decrease. The probability of an increase is largely driven by random fluctuations in deposit weighting (which caused a tiny bounce in mid-2025), but the macro environment is strongly biased against an increase. I place the probability for the first resolution date low, as it may just capture the November or early December reading (likely flat or lower), and slightly higher for the second date to reflect increased uncertainty over a longer horizon.
